description: Native app surfaces phase A4 (docs/design/native-app-surfaces.md): iOS app contexts run end-to-end on a managed simulator via the lazily-installed XCUITest driver. startSurface resolves/boots a simulator through simctl (newest iPhone by default, or a named/created device), find/click/screenshot act on it through the XCUITest semantic mapping, and closeSurface terminates the app; the run-end sweep shuts down only simulators Doc Detective booted. Gated to iOS targets via runOn; on Windows/Linux every test lands SKIPPED with actionable macOS/Xcode/simctl guidance. The first XCUITest session on a cold runner builds WebDriverAgent via xcodebuild (minutes), so startSurface timeouts are generous. Built-in stock apps (Settings, Calendar) are used so no app payload is fetched.

tests.description: A5 remains gated: iOS mobile-browser contexts SKIP with the phase A5 message.

Launch Settings by bundle ID on the default (newest installed iPhone) simulator, wait for a stable row to exist, find it through the XCUITest column, screenshot the app, then close it. The real end-to-end PASS on a capable macOS host; SKIPs elsewhere.

Name a device that does not exist so Doc Detective creates it via simctl (create-boot path) using the newest iPhone device type + runtime, launches Settings on it, and shuts it down at run end (bootedByUs). Exercises the named-device create path distinct from the default-device reuse path above.

Two stock apps (Settings + Calendar) share one simulator session; acting on each surface foregrounds it via activateApp, proving multi-app-per-simulator switching. Each surface is screenshotted (which forces the foreground switch) and then closed.
